content.text: The most common failure mode in enterprise GenAI programs is not technical — it is organizational: the right people are not making the right decisions. Product owners ship high-risk use cases without governance sign-off because nobody defined that governance sign-off was required. Platform teams build infrastructure that BUs don't adopt because nobody assigned accountability for adoption. RACI matrices exist precisely to prevent this class of failure by making explicit what is implicit.

attrs.alt: GenAI Decision Rights Escalation Flowchart: new decisions route through three diamonds — policy/binding standard (→ Exec Steering), Tier 2 risk gate triggered (→ AI Risk Review Panel), platform/standards change (→ Tech Review Board). All other decisions stay with the BU guild.

content.text: Enterprise GenAI programs involve more roles than most teams anticipate. Many of these roles overlap with existing job titles — the question is not whether these people exist, but whether their GenAI-specific accountability is explicit:
GenAI strategy ratification; budget authority; political cover for the CoE charter
Policy approval; charter renewal; quarterly OKR review; escalation endpoint for CoE
CoE operations; P&L; hiring; executive relationship; Tier 2 gate accountability
Technical standards; model evaluation; platform architecture decisions; TRB chair
Responsible AI framework; bias audit methodology; high-risk use case review; training on ethics
LLM gateway; model registry; deployment pipelines; observability; platform uptime
Intake triage; governance cadence; metrics; charter version management
BU intake pre-screening; CoE liaison; local standards adoption; BU use-case registry
Use-case definition; success metrics; user acceptance; production deployment sign-off
Guild resourcing; technical quality bar for BU GenAI features; guild lead's solid-line manager
Regulatory opinion on use cases; acceptable-use policy input; Tier 2 gate participant
AI threat model; prompt injection testing; model exfiltration controls
GDPR/HIPAA compliance for prompts and training data; consent gating requirements
Master RACI for GenAI Program Decisions

The following RACI covers the decisions that generate the most organizational conflict in GenAI programs. Work through this matrix with your leadership team before standing up the CoE — alignment on paper prevents fights in production.

RACI key: R = Responsible (does the work), A = Accountable (owns the outcome; one person only), C = Consulted (provides input before decision), I = Informed (notified after decision). A cell can hold R+A when one person is both accountable and executes the work.
Set rate limits and cost caps
Reject or suspend a production use case
Approve new platform service for CoE catalog
Upgrade LLM gateway to new major version
Add new vector store or embedding service
Define eval benchmark for a use-case type
Approve fine-tuned model for BU use
Deploy a BU GenAI feature to production
Grant CoE certification to an engineer
Approve new BU guild lead candidate

Beyond the RACI, it is useful to describe decision rights categorically — the types of decisions the CoE can make unilaterally, the decisions that require cross-functional consensus, and the decisions that belong to BUs alone:
CoE Unilateral Authority (No BU Approval Required)
Maintaining the approved model registry: adding and removing models
Setting and enforcing rate limits on the LLM gateway
Updating the PII guardrail ruleset and prompt injection filters
Defining the evaluation runbook and quality benchmark thresholds
Requiring certifications as a prerequisite for guild membership
Convening the Risk Review Panel for any Tier 2 use case
These decisions are binding by charter. BUs may object through the escalation path (CoE Lead → Exec Steering Committee) but may not bypass the decision unilaterally.
Approving a new use-case type that is not covered by existing policy (creates precedent)
Any use case that processes data classified Confidential or Restricted
Deploying to external users for the first time in a new jurisdiction
Integrating a model provider with no existing data processing agreement
Enabling a new agent tool category (file system access, code execution, external API calls)
Choosing which approved models to use within a use case
Designing prompt templates and system messages
Defining success metrics and eval criteria for their use case
Setting their own deployment cadence and rollout strategy
Choosing retrieval strategies (chunking, embedding model, reranking) within approved options
Staffing their guild team (within guidelines on guild lead qualifications)

Publishing this three-part list to all engineering teams removes 80% of CoE intake friction: engineers stop submitting advisory requests for decisions that are already theirs to make, and stop unilaterally making decisions that require a gate review.
RACI matrices are routinely authored and then ignored. The failure modes follow predictable patterns:
Multiple A cells for one decision
Both the CoE Lead and the BU Product Owner are marked A; neither feels truly accountable
RACI rule: exactly one A per decision. If two people claim accountability, escalate to the Exec Steering Committee to resolve before the matrix is published
Everyone is C or R; no A
Reviews happen but nobody acts on them; decisions drift
Before publishing, identify the A for every decision. If you can't name a person, the governance structure is incomplete
Legal is always C but never reviewed
Legal becomes a checkbox; their C is not substantive
Legal's C means a specific named person reviewed and confirmed. Use async approval with a documented response, not a passive opt-out
RACI covers process decisions but not technical decisions
Platform engineers make architecture changes without triggering TRB review; BUs build on unstable base
Maintain separate RACI rows for technical decisions (model selection, platform version, eval benchmark) — these are as important as process decisions
RACI is a document, not a system
Engineers don't know the RACI exists; decisions are made ad-hoc
Encode decision routing in the intake form triage logic (see code below); the RACI drives the system, the system drives behavior
Encoding RACI in the Intake System
Role Accountability in Practice: Worked Examples
Example 1: BU wants to use a new model not in the registry
A BU engineer discovers GPT-4o mini and wants to use it for a low-cost summarization task. Walk through the RACI:
BU Guild Lead pre-screens: the model is not in the approved registry → triggers CoE intake
CoE PM triages: model registry addition is a CoE Unilateral decision (Principal Architect is R/A)
Principal Architect runs a 3-day model evaluation: capability benchmarks, cost per token, data residency, provider DPA status
Principal Architect approves and adds to registry, classifies as approved for Internal data classification
CoE PM notifies BU Guild Lead; BU proceeds with their use case
Example 2: BU wants to build a customer-facing AI assistant
A product team wants to deploy a GenAI assistant on their customer portal. Walk through the RACI:
BU Guild Lead pre-screens: external user-facing → Tier 2 trigger
CoE PM triages as Tier 2; convenes Risk Review Panel within 5 business days
Risk Review Panel: CoE Lead (chairs), AI Ethics Lead (reviews output bias risk), Legal (reviews GDPR consent gating) review the use-case memo
Panel approves with conditions: PII guardrail required on all prompts; human review for output before high-stakes recommendations; rate limit 1000 calls/day until behavior monitored
BU Product Owner signs off on conditions; BU Engineering Lead deploys with conditions enforced
Use case logged in AI risk register with quarterly re-review date
Example 3: CoE wants to deprecate an old model version
The Principal Architect wants to remove an older model from the registry because the provider will deprecate it next quarter:
Principal Architect identifies all BU use cases using the model via the registry (the use-case database links use cases to models)
CoE PM notifies all affected BU Guild Leads with 60-day deprecation notice
Principal Architect proposes the recommended replacement model (CoE Unilateral — no approval needed)
Guild leads for affected BUs raise any compatibility concerns at the next guild sync
MLOps Lead coordinates migration; Principal Architect approves the deprecation timeline
Exec Steering Committee is Informed in the next quarterly report
Stakeholder Alignment: Getting the RACI Ratified

The RACI is only effective if the stakeholders listed in it have accepted their roles. A RACI authored by the CoE and published without stakeholder review is a political liability, not a governance tool. Three practices make ratification work:
Before circulating the full matrix, identify the three or four cells most likely to generate pushback. Common contested decisions:

— BU product owners often want A here (they own the product), while the CoE needs A to maintain governance authority. Resolution: CoE has A for the compliance determination; BU Product Owner has A for the product launch decision after compliance approval
"Deploy a BU GenAI feature to production"
— CoE architects sometimes want A here. Resist: this creates a CoE deployment bottleneck. BU Engineering Lead must be A; CoE is C for platform compliance checks only

— Legal often wants A here. The policy is a legal document, but the technical accuracy requires CoE authorship. Compromise: CoE Lead is R (authors the policy), Exec Steering is A (ratifies), Legal is C (reviews for regulatory compliance)
2. Use a decision log during the workshop

# Template for capturing RACI workshop decisions
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date

@dataclass
class RACIWorkshopDecision:
    decision_label: str
    proposed_raci: dict[str, str]    # role -> RACI letter
    contested_by: list[str]          # stakeholder names
    final_raci: dict[str, str]
    rationale: str
    agreed_on: date
    approved_by: list[str]           # must include all contested stakeholders

# Example — the contested "deploy to production" decision
decisions = [
    RACIWorkshopDecision(
        decision_label="Deploy a BU GenAI feature to production",
        proposed_raci={
            "CoE Lead": "C",
            "Principal Architect": "C",
            "BU Engineering Lead": "A",
            "BU Product Owner": "R",
            "MLOps Lead": "C",
        },
        contested_by=["Principal Architect"],  # wanted A
        final_raci={
            "CoE Lead": "I",
            "Principal Architect": "C",          # platform compliance check only
            "BU Engineering Lead": "A",          # owns the deployment
            "BU Product Owner": "R",
            "MLOps Lead": "C",
        },
        rationale=(
            "Principal Architect's A would create a deployment bottleneck. "
            "Their C is limited to verifying LLM gateway integration; "
            "BU Engineering Lead is accountable for the deployment itself."
        ),
        agreed_on=date(2024, 3, 15),
        approved_by=["Principal Architect", "BU Engineering Lead", "CoE Lead"],
    )
]
3. Version and publish with an amendment process
The RACI is a living document. Publish version 1.0 with a clear amendment process:
Any stakeholder may propose an amendment to a RACI cell
Amendments are reviewed at the quarterly guild sync; changes affecting governance decisions go to Exec Steering
Each published version is timestamped and archived; all intake decisions link to the RACI version in effect at time of decision (for audit trail)
Responsibility Assignment for Common GenAI Incidents
Incident response is where RACI ambiguity causes the most damage. Define it before you need it:
CoE Lead notifies Exec Steering if > 1h
Legal drafts external statement if regulatory notification required
CoE PM → affected BU guild leads
CoE Lead + CISO if data exfiltration confirmed
BU bypassed Tier 2 gate for high-risk use case
BU Engineering Lead + BU Product Owner notified
Exec Steering + Legal; BU may need to suspend the feature
Legal drafts customer and regulatory notification
Exec Steering immediately; CEO if enterprise customer data affected

Pre-defining incident accountabilities means the first 10 minutes of a real incident are spent on diagnosis and containment, not on resolving who is in charge. The Incident Commander role should be assigned to a named person per incident type — not to a team — because teams cannot be paged.

The RACI matrix is the contractual layer of the GenAI operating model. Without it, governance is improvised; with it, every stakeholder knows exactly when they need to act, when they need to be consulted, and when they can move autonomously. The matrix is most valuable as a decision-routing system encoded in the intake process, not as a document living in a shared drive.
Define the role taxonomy first: 13+ roles in an enterprise GenAI program, each with distinct accountability that must not overlap
Publish a RACI covering governance, platform, and enablement decisions separately — they require different combinations of stakeholders
Enforce the RACI rule: exactly one A per decision; if you can't name an accountable person, the governance structure is not yet designed
Categorize all decisions into CoE Unilateral, Consensus Required, or BU Autonomous — publish this list to all engineering teams to reduce CoE intake friction
Encode decision routing in the intake triage system so the RACI drives behavior, not just documentation
